title: Untitled
research_problem: The exponential growth of research articles makes manual literature review increasingly time-consuming, tedious, and error-prone, creating a need for automated systems that can generate literature review segments from PDF inputs
method_summary: Three approaches are compared for automated literature review generation: (1) Frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy to calculate sentence weights based on word frequency and select top 10% sentences; (2) Fine-tuned T5 transformer model trained on SciTLDR dataset for abstractive summarization; (3) Retrieval-augmented generation using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base and custom prompt engineering
method_family: Extractive Summarization; Seq2Seq Transformer; Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
tasks: Automated Literature Review Generation; Scientific Document Summarization
claim_text: GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with RAG achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming both T5 (0.268) and spaCy (0.257)
limitations: The ROUGE-L score for LLM (0.181) is lower than T5 (0.204), indicating potential issues with longest common subsequence overlap | The frequency-based spaCy approach achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, with ROUGE-2 at only 0.055 | No training was performed for spaCy approach - only used for extractive summarization testing | Limited to English scientific documents in the SciTLDR dataset; applicability to other languages and document types not validated
notes: The paper implements three complete end-to-end pipelines where users can directly generate literature reviews from PDF inputs. A GUI tool was created for the best-performing LLM-based system. The comparison contributes to understanding the effectiveness of different NLP approaches for literature review automation.

research_problem: The exponential growth of research articles makes manual literature review increasingly time-consuming, tedious, and error-prone, creating a need for automated systems that can generate literature review segments from PDF inputs
method_summary: Three approaches are compared for automated literature review generation: (1) Frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy to calculate sentence weights based on word frequency and select top 10% sentences; (2) Fine-tuned T5 transformer model trained on SciTLDR dataset for abstractive summarization; (3) Retrieval-augmented generation using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base and custom prompt engineering
method_family: Extractive Summarization; Seq2Seq Transformer; Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
tasks: Automated Literature Review Generation; Scientific Document Summarization
claim_text: The LLM-based approach produces literature reviews with better unigram and bigram overlap with human summaries compared to transformer and frequency-based methods
limitations: The ROUGE-L score for LLM (0.181) is lower than T5 (0.204), indicating potential issues with longest common subsequence overlap | The frequency-based spaCy approach achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, with ROUGE-2 at only 0.055 | No training was performed for spaCy approach - only used for extractive summarization testing | Limited to English scientific documents in the SciTLDR dataset; applicability to other languages and document types not validated
notes: The paper implements three complete end-to-end pipelines where users can directly generate literature reviews from PDF inputs. A GUI tool was created for the best-performing LLM-based system. The comparison contributes to understanding the effectiveness of different NLP approaches for literature review automation.

title: Untitled
research_problem: The exponential growth of research articles makes manual literature review increasingly time-consuming, tedious, and error-prone, creating a need for automated systems that can generate literature review segments from PDF inputs
method_summary: Three approaches are compared for automated literature review generation: (1) Frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy to calculate sentence weights based on word frequency and select top 10% sentences; (2) Fine-tuned T5 transformer model trained on SciTLDR dataset for abstractive summarization; (3) Retrieval-augmented generation using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base and custom prompt engineering

limitations: The ROUGE-L score for LLM (0.181) is lower than T5 (0.204), indicating potential issues with longest common subsequence overlap | The frequency-based spaCy approach achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, with ROUGE-2 at only 0.055 | No training was performed for spaCy approach - only used for extractive summarization testing | Limited to English scientific documents in the SciTLDR dataset; applicability to other languages and document types not validated
notes: The paper implements three complete end-to-end pipelines where users can directly generate literature reviews from PDF inputs. A GUI tool was created for the best-performing LLM-based system. The comparison contributes to understanding the effectiveness of different NLP approaches for literature review automation.

title: Untitled
research_problem: The exponential growth of research articles makes manual literature review increasingly time-consuming, tedious, and error-prone, creating a need for automated systems that can generate literature review segments from PDF inputs
method_summary: Three approaches are compared for automated literature review generation: (1) Frequency-based extractive summarization using spaCy to calculate sentence weights based on word frequency and select top 10% sentences; (2) Fine-tuned T5 transformer model trained on SciTLDR dataset for abstractive summarization; (3) Retrieval-augmented generation using GPT-3.5-TURBO-0125 with SciTLDR as knowledge base and custom prompt engineering
method_family: Extractive Summarization; Seq2Seq Transformer; Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
tasks: Automated Literature Review Generation; Scientific Document Summarization
claim_text: The T5 transformer model trained on SciTLDR achieves second-place performance with ROUGE-1 of 0.268 and ROUGE-L of 0.204
limitations: The ROUGE-L score for LLM (0.181) is lower than T5 (0.204), indicating potential issues with longest common subsequence overlap | The frequency-based spaCy approach achieved the lowest scores across all metrics, with ROUGE-2 at only 0.055 | No training was performed for spaCy approach - only used for extractive summarization testing | Limited to English scientific documents in the SciTLDR dataset; applicability to other languages and document types not validated
notes: The paper implements three complete end-to-end pipelines where users can directly generate literature reviews from PDF inputs. A GUI tool was created for the best-performing LLM-based system. The comparison contributes to understanding the effectiveness of different NLP approaches for literature review automation.
